title: Positive news for the Chinese economy
section_path: Positive news for the Chinese economy
section_path_text: Positive news for the Chinese economy
summary:
Main
The Baltic dry Index remained in the black for a sixth consecutive week, albeit with a relatively modest weekly gain as the capesizes offset a robust performance for the panamaxes. The commodities had a week with many sizeable moves, with a steep decline for iron ore among the more notable developments. By Ulf Bergman

Macro/Geopolitics
The new week has begun with positive news for the Chinese economy.

children.title: Main
children.summary: The Baltic dry Index remained in the black for a sixth consecutive week, albeit with a relatively modest weekly gain as the capesizes offset a robust performance for the panamaxes. The commodities had a week with many sizeable moves, with a steep decline for iron ore among the more notable developments.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: The new week has begun with positive news for the Chinese economy. According to data released earlier today, the country's industrial production expanded by more than expected during the year's first two months.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: Crude oil bounced back last week after the International Energy Agency published a bullish demand outlook. Despite remaining broadly unchanged on Friday, the May Brent futures recorded a weekly gain of 4.0 per cent as they settled at 85.34 dollars per barrel.

children.title: Freight and Bunker Markets
children.summary: The Baltic Dry Index advanced by 1.2 per cent over the past five sessions, a sixth consecutive week in the black. However, the mildly positive reading hid considerable divergence among the vessel segments.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: Grains and oilseed have faced significant headwinds since the beginning of the year as a bullish global supply outlook has weighed on prices. While corn and soybeans have recovered some of the losses recently amid downgrades to expected harvest sizes, the May futures for the two crops listed on the CBOT are currently trading around ten per cent below the levels at the end of December.
